items.title: Blue Origin rocket explodes during test in setback for Jeff Bezos
items.summary: STORY: :: Courtney BlairThis uncrewed Blue Origin rocket had just ignited in its launchpad on Thursday when it suddenly exploded...erupting into a massive fireball, sending a towering plume of flames and smoke into the air.Jeff Bezos' space venture said it had experienced an “anomaly,” a term commonly used to describe a launch failure or explosion.In a post on X, the Amazon billionaire said all personnel were accounted for and that it was "too early to know the root cause" of the incident.NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman said the agency would work with partners to support an investigation, adding that NASA would provide information on any impact to its Artemis and Moon Base programs.The incident is a major setback for Bezos' Blue Origin, as it seeks to narrow the gap with Elon Musk’s IPO-bound SpaceX.Both billionaire-run companies have been racing to help return people to the moon by designing the lunar landers NASA will use.Musk responded on X to a video of the Blue Origin explosion, saying: “Most unfortunate. Rockets are hard."

items.title: The Week in Numbers: Temu fined, Micron's massive valuation
items.summary: STORY: From a big fine for Temu to Micron's massive valuation.:: Micron:: The Week In NumbersThis is The Week in Numbers.:: $232 million$232 million dollars is how much Chinese online retailer Temu has been fined by the European Union.EU tech regulators say the company failed to stop the sale of illegal products under the Digital Services Act.The Act requires large online companies to do more to tackle illegal and harmful content on their platforms.In a statement Temu says it respects the DSA objectives but disagrees with the Commission's decision. :: $1.43 billion$1.43 billion is the amount of damages the Australian government is seeking in its lawsuit against U.S. chemical maker 3M.That's after its firefighting foam it supplied contained so-called "forever chemicals'.It's the country's largest ever legal claim after the foam was used at 28 defense bases across the country. 3M said it would defend itself against the claims in court. :: $150 billion$150 billion a year is what Nvidia's chief executive said the chip company plans to invest in Taiwan.CEO Jensen Huang termed it the "epicenter" of the AI revolution and predicted it will be the world's tech manufacturing hub for a long time.His comments came at the launch celebration in Taipei for the chipmaker's planned Taiwan headquarters.:: $1 trillion:: Micron$1 trillion is the market value chipmaker Micron Technology topped for the first time.It crowns a dizzying rally that has cemented the largest U.S. memory chipmaker as one of the standout winners of the AI boom. Micron's shares rose more than 18% on Tuesday, pushing the stock to a record high.:: 8%:: Ferrari8% is the plunge Ferrari shares took on Tuesday after the auto icon unveiled it's first electric vehicle.The five-seat sedan bears little resemblance to Ferraris of the past, drawing a cool response from fans, and investors. CEO Benedetto Vigna defended the new model and said the company is receiving "strong interest" from new and existing customers.

items.title: Samsung ships faster HBM4E chip samples to customers; shares jump
items.summary: STORY: Samsung Electronics made a big move in the AI chip race on Friday.It said it's started shipping samples of its latest high-bandwidth memory - or HBM - chip to customers.It pulls the South Korean tech giant ahead of rivals as they compete to send out new versions of HBMs which are critical to AI data centers.The new chip is the 12-layer HBM4E and Samsung said it's more than 20% faster than its previous-generation HBM4 products.The rollout is part of Samsung's efforts to regain momentum in the HBM market.It had fallen behind rivals like SK Hynix and Micron in supplying advanced AI memory chips, particularly to Nvidia.Samsung's clients include major AI players Nvidia, AMD and Google, among others.Demand has surged for advanced memory chips used in AI servers and processors.Investors liked what they heard Friday.Shares of Samsung Electronics rose as much as 6.5% in morning trading.Rival SK Hynix also saw its shares climb just over 1%.

items.title: Analysis-In China, AMD CEO Lisa Su is understated while Nvidia's Huang is more razzmatazz
items.summary: When AMD CEO Lisa Su arrived in China last week just days after Nvidia's CEO left, she kept a much lower profile than Jensen Huang, who drew crowds in the centre of the capital as he posed for photos and ate local ‌delicacies like Beijing-style soybean paste noodles.  Just a year ago at the annual Computex trade show in Taipei, Huang said Nvidia's market share in China had dropped to 50% from 95% due to U.S. export controls.  Since then, that ​has effectively fallen to zero, he said this year, amid Beijing's push for self-reliance in advanced AI chips.

Nvidia CEO Jensen ‌Huang, arriving in Taipei more than a week ahead of the show, more than made that point when he said on ‌Wednesday that his company would spend as much as $150 billion a year in Taiwan, which he called the epicentre of the AI revolution.  AMD CEO Lisa Su also said last week that the company would invest more than $10 billion in Taiwan's AI sector, adding that it was co-investing with Taiwanese partners to ensure sufficient capacity for expansion in 2026 and beyond.

items.title: Dell shares rocket on bullish forecast for AI demand
items.summary: STORY: :: DellDell shares rocketed higher in after-hours trade Thursday, jumping around 39%.The surge came after the computer maker lifted revenue and profit expectations.Dell says it is seeing soaring demand for AI-optimized servers used in data centers.It’s benefiting as tech giants like Alphabet and Amazon plan to spend over $700 billion on AI-related infrastructure this year.Dell now expects annual revenue of up to $169 billion - way up on the previous forecast of up to $142 billion.AI server revenue is expected to hit $60 billion for fiscal 2027, $10 billion up on previous expectations.The company has been adjusting prices constantly as strong global demand causes the cost of memory chips to soar.:: DellOn a call with analysts, Chief Operating Officer Jeff Clarke said he knew customers were feeling the pain.But he said there was little sign of inflationary pressure easing.One analyst told Reuters that Dell looked well placed to cope with the chip shortage, helped by its scale and strong relationships with suppliers.
